1	---
2	title: TOML
3	description: Use Bun's built-in support for TOML files through both runtime APIs and bundler integration
4	---
5	
6	In Bun, TOML is a first-class citizen alongside JSON, JSON5, and YAML. You can:
7	
8	- Parse TOML strings with `Bun.TOML.parse`
9	- `import` & `require` TOML files as modules at runtime (including hot reloading & watch mode support)
10	- `import` & `require` TOML files in frontend apps via Bun's bundler
11	
12	---
13	
14	## Runtime API
15	
16	### `Bun.TOML.parse()`
17	
18	Parse a TOML string into a JavaScript object.
19	
20	```ts
21	import { TOML } from "bun";
22	const text = `
23	name = "my-app"
24	version = "1.0.0"
25	debug = true
26	
27	[database]
28	host = "localhost"
29	port = 5432
30	
31	[features]
32	tags = ["web", "api"]
33	`;
34	
35	const data = TOML.parse(text);
36	console.log(data);
37	// {
38	//   name: "my-app",
39	//   version: "1.0.0",
40	//   debug: true,
41	//   database: { host: "localhost", port: 5432 },
42	//   features: { tags: ["web", "api"] }
43	// }
44	```
45	
46	#### Supported TOML Features
47	
48	Bun's TOML parser supports the [TOML v1.0 specification](https://toml.io/en/v1.0.0), including:
49	
50	- **Strings**: basic (`"..."`) and literal (`'...'`), including multi-line
51	- **Integers**: decimal, hex (`0x`), octal (`0o`), and binary (`0b`)
52	- **Floats**: including `inf` and `nan`
53	- **Booleans**: `true` and `false`
54	- **Arrays**: including mixed types and nested arrays
55	- **Tables**: standard (`[table]`) and inline (`{ key = "value" }`)
56	- **Array of tables**: `[[array]]`
57	- **Dotted keys**: `a.b.c = "value"`
58	- **Comments**: using `#`
59	
60	```ts
61	const data = Bun.TOML.parse(`
62	# Application config
63	title = "My App"
64	
65	[owner]
66	name = "John Doe"
67	
68	[database]
69	enabled = true
70	ports = [8000, 8001, 8002]
71	connection_max = 5000
72	
73	[servers.alpha]
74	ip = "10.0.0.1"
75	role = "frontend"
76	
77	[servers.beta]
78	ip = "10.0.0.2"
79	role = "backend"
80	`);
81	```
82	
83	#### Error Handling
84	
85	`Bun.TOML.parse()` throws if the TOML is invalid:
86	
87	```ts
88	try {
89	  Bun.TOML.parse("invalid = = =");
90	} catch (error) {
91	  console.error("Failed to parse TOML:", error.message);
92	}
93	```
94	
95	---
96	
97	## Module Import
98	
99	### ES Modules
100	
101	You can import TOML files directly as ES modules. The TOML content is parsed and made available as both default and named exports:
102	
103	```toml config.toml
104	[database]
105	host = "localhost"
106	port = 5432
107	name = "myapp"
108	
109	[redis]
110	host = "localhost"
111	port = 6379
112	
113	[features]
114	auth = true
115	rateLimit = true
116	analytics = false
117	```
118	
119	#### Default Import
120	
121	```ts app.ts icon="/icons/typescript.svg"
122	import config from "./config.toml";
123	
124	console.log(config.database.host); // "localhost"
125	console.log(config.redis.port); // 6379
126	```
127	
128	#### Named Imports
129	
130	You can destructure top-level TOML tables as named imports:
131	
132	```ts app.ts icon="/icons/typescript.svg"
133	import { database, redis, features } from "./config.toml";
134	
135	console.log(database.host); // "localhost"
136	console.log(redis.port); // 6379
137	console.log(features.auth); // true
138	```
139	
140	Or combine both:
141	
142	```ts app.ts icon="/icons/typescript.svg"
143	import config, { database, features } from "./config.toml";
144	
145	// Use the full config object
146	console.log(config);
147	
148	// Or use specific parts
149	if (features.rateLimit) {
150	  setupRateLimiting(database);
151	}
152	```
153	
154	#### Import Attributes
155	
156	You can also use import attributes to load any file as TOML:
157	
158	```ts app.ts icon="/icons/typescript.svg"
159	import myConfig from "./my.config" with { type: "toml" };
160	```
161	
162	### CommonJS
163	
164	TOML files can also be required in CommonJS:
165	
166	```ts app.ts icon="/icons/typescript.svg"
167	const config = require("./config.toml");
168	console.log(config.database.name); // "myapp"
169	
170	// Destructuring also works
171	const { database, redis } = require("./config.toml");
172	console.log(database.port); // 5432
173	```
174	
175	---
176	
177	## Hot Reloading with TOML
178	
179	When you run your application with `bun --hot`, changes to TOML files are automatically detected and reloaded without restarting:
180	
181	```toml config.toml
182	[server]
183	port = 3000
184	host = "localhost"
185	
186	[features]
187	debug = true
188	verbose = false
189	```
190	
191	```ts server.ts icon="/icons/typescript.svg"
192	import { server, features } from "./config.toml";
193	
194	console.log(`Starting server on ${server.host}:${server.port}`);
195	
196	Bun.serve({
197	  port: server.port,
198	  hostname: server.host,
199	  fetch(req) {
200	    if (features.verbose) {
201	      console.log(`${req.method} ${req.url}`);
202	    }
203	    return new Response("Hello World");
204	  },
205	});
206	```
207	
208	Run with hot reloading:
209	
210	```bash terminal icon="terminal"
211	bun --hot server.ts
212	```
213	
214	Now when you modify `config.toml`, the changes are immediately reflected in your running application.
215	
216	---
217	
218	## Bundler Integration
219	
220	When you import TOML files and bundle with Bun, the TOML is parsed at build time and included as a JavaScript module:
221	
222	```bash terminal icon="terminal"
223	bun build app.ts --outdir=dist
224	```
225	
226	This means:
227	
228	- Zero runtime TOML parsing overhead in production
229	- Smaller bundle sizes
230	- Tree-shaking support for unused properties (named imports)
231	
232	### Dynamic Imports
233	
234	TOML files can be dynamically imported:
235	
236	```ts
237	const config = await import("./config.toml");
238	```

## Comprehensive Exploration: Bun Global Object Construction and Namespace Properties

I've thoroughly explored the Bun codebase to understand how the `Bun` global object is constructed and how namespace properties (like TOML, YAML, JSONC, JSON5) are attached to it. Here's the complete architecture:

### **1. High-Level Architecture**

The `Bun` global object uses a **lazy property callback pattern** where format namespace objects are created on-demand when accessed, rather than being instantiated upfront. This is efficient for performance.

### **2. Key Files to Modify for Adding a New Format Namespace (e.g., INI)**

#### **A. Create the Namespace Object File**
**Location:** `src/bun.js/api/INIObject.zig`

Pattern example from existing code:
- **TOMLObject** (`src/bun.js/api/TOMLObject.zig`): 79 lines - Simple structure with `create()` and `parse()` functions
- **YAMLObject** (`src/bun.js/api/YAMLObject.zig`): 1095 lines - More complex with `create()`, `parse()`, and `stringify()`
- **JSON5Object** (`src/bun.js/api/JSON5Object.zig`): 434 lines - With `create()`, `parse()`, and `stringify()`
- **JSONCObject** (`src/bun.js/api/JSONCObject.zig`): 65 lines - Simple with just `parse()`
- **MarkdownObject** (`src/bun.js/api/MarkdownObject.zig`): Multiple functions like `html()`, `render()`, `react()`

**Structure pattern:**
```zig
pub fn create(globalThis: *jsc.JSGlobalObject) jsc.JSValue {
    const object = JSValue.createEmptyObject(globalThis, N); // N = number of methods
    object.put(
        globalThis,
        ZigString.static("methodName"),
        jsc.JSFunction.create(globalThis, "methodName", methodImpl, argCount, .{}),
    );
    return object;
}

pub fn methodImpl(
    globalThis: *jsc.JSGlobalObject,
    callframe: *jsc.CallFrame,
) bun.JSError!jsc.JSValue {
    // Parse input, process, return JSValue
}
```

#### **B. Update BunObject.zig**
**Location:** `src/bun.js/api/BunObject.zig` (2098 lines)

**Three sections to update:**

1. **Lines 66-70:** Add lazy property callback declaration
```zig
pub const INI = toJSLazyPropertyCallback(Bun.getINIObject);
```

2. **Lines 136-140:** Export the lazy property callback
```zig
@export(&BunObject.INI, .{ .name = lazyPropertyCallbackName("INI") });
```

3. **Lines 1273-1289:** Add getter function
```zig
pub fn getINIObject(globalThis: *jsc.JSGlobalObject, _: *jsc.JSObject) jsc.JSValue {
    return INIObject.create(globalThis);
}
```

4. **Lines 2078-2083:** Add import at end of file
```zig
const INIObject = bun.api.INIObject;
```

#### **C. Update BunObject.cpp**
**Location:** `src/bun.js/bindings/BunObject.cpp` (18,992 lines)

**In the @begin bunObjectTable section (lines 945-1057):**
Add one line (around line 968):
```cpp
    INI                                            BunObject_lazyPropCb_wrap_INI                                      DontDelete|PropertyCallback
```

This is a lookup table that defines JSC properties for the Bun object.

#### **D. Update BunObject+exports.h**
**Location:** `src/bun.js/bindings/BunObject+exports.h` (100 lines)

**In the FOR_EACH_GETTER macro (lines 5-41):**
Add the property name:
```cpp
    macro(INI) \
```

This header file generates C++ wrappers for lazy property callbacks. It expands macros to create function declarations and wrapper definitions.

#### **E. Update api.zig**
**Location:** `src/bun.js/api.zig` (62 lines)

Add export after line 34:
```zig
pub const INIObject = @import("./api/INIObject.zig");
```

This makes the INIObject available as `bun.api.INIObject` throughout the codebase.

### **3. Test File Pattern**

**Examples:**
- `test/js/bun/resolve/toml/toml.test.js` - TOML tests (50+ lines)
- `test/js/bun/yaml/yaml.test.ts` - YAML tests (comprehensive)
- `test/js/bun/resolve/yaml/yaml.test.js` - YAML bundler tests

**Test pattern:**
```typescript
import { INI } from "bun";
import { test, expect } from "bun:test";

test("INI.parse parses INI string", () => {
    const result = INI.parse("key=value\nsection.key=value");
    expect(result).toEqual({ key: "value", section: { key: "value" } });
});
```

Run with: `bun bd test test/path/to/test.test.ts`

### **4. Existing INI Parser**

There IS already an INI parser in the codebase:
- **Location:** `src/ini.zig` (substantial implementation, 400+ lines)
- **Key Components:**
  - `Parser` struct with initialization and parsing
  - Handles sections with `[section]` syntax
  - Parses key-value pairs
  - Error handling with logger integration
  - Environment variable expansion support

This parser can be directly integrated into an `INIObject` namespace similar to TOML/YAML.

### **5. How Lazy Property Callbacks Work**

The pattern used in BunObject.zig:
```zig
const LazyPropertyCallback = fn (*jsc.JSGlobalObject, *jsc.JSObject) callconv(jsc.conv) JSValue;

fn toJSLazyPropertyCallback(comptime wrapped: anytype) LazyPropertyCallback {
    return struct {
        pub fn callback(this: *jsc.JSGlobalObject, object: *jsc.JSObject) callconv(jsc.conv) JSValue {
            return bun.jsc.toJSHostCall(this, @src(), wrapped, .{ this, object });
        }
    }.callback;
}
```

When `Bun.INI` is accessed for the first time, the callback fires, calls `getINIObject()`, which calls `INIObject.create()`, creating the namespace object on-demand.

### **6. Property Definition Flow**

1. **Zig code** (BunObject.zig) defines property callbacks
2. **@export** makes them available to C++
3. **BunObject.cpp** includes generated header (BunObject.lut.h) created from the @begin bunObjectTable section
4. **BunObject+exports.h** generates wrapper functions that call the Zig functions from C++
5. **JSC property table** (bunObjectTable) registers these as lazy properties
6. When accessed from JS, JSC calls the lazy property callback, which calls the Zig function, which creates and returns the object

### **7. Complete File List to Modify**

To add a new format namespace (e.g., INI):

1. **Create:** `src/bun.js/api/INIObject.zig` ✓
2. **Modify:** `src/bun.js/api/BunObject.zig` (3 sections) ✓
3. **Modify:** `src/bun.js/bindings/BunObject.cpp` (1 line in lookup table) ✓
4. **Modify:** `src/bun.js/bindings/BunObject+exports.h` (1 line in macro) ✓
5. **Modify:** `src/bun.js/api.zig` (1 line export) ✓
6. **Create tests:** `test/js/bun/ini/ini.test.ts` (optional but recommended) ✓

### **8. Example: Integrating INI Parser**

The INI parser at `src/ini.zig` exports:
- `Parser` struct
- Typical flow: create parser → parse → deinit

An `INIObject` would follow the TOML pattern:
```zig
pub fn parse(globalThis: *jsc.JSGlobalObject, callframe: *jsc.CallFrame) bun.JSError!jsc.JSValue {
    // Get input string/buffer
    // Create ini.Parser
    // Parse using parser.parse()
    // Convert result to JS value
    // Return JS object
}
```

### **9. CodeGen Information**

- There are `.classes.ts` files in `src/bun.js/api/` (e.g., BunObject.classes.ts)
- These define class structures for code generation
- Most format objects don't need classes.ts as they're simpler (just namespace objects with methods)
- Complex classes like Subprocess, Archive use the classes.ts pattern

---

All relevant files have been identified and their purposes explained. The architecture is consistent and follows the same pattern for all format namespace objects (TOML, YAML, JSON5, JSONC, Markdown).

input.content:
# Plan: Add `Bun.INI` Namespace

## Context

Bun exposes format namespaces (`Bun.TOML`, `Bun.JSONC`, `Bun.JSON5`, `Bun.YAML`) as public APIs for parsing/stringifying various data formats. This change adds a new `Bun.INI` namespace with `parse` and `stringify` methods, following the exact same lazy-loading pattern. The INI parser must be self-contained (not reusing the internal npm INI parser at `src/ini.zig` which is coupled to `bun.DotEnv.Loader` for env var expansion).

## Files to Modify

### 1. Create `src/bun.js/api/INIObject.zig` (NEW)

The main implementation file. Follow the JSON5Object.zig pattern (most similar since it has both parse and stringify, and directly converts to/from JS values).

**`create()` function:**
- Create an empty JS object with 2 properties
- Register `parse` (arity 1) and `stringify` (arity 3) as JSFunctions

**`parse()` function:**
- Accept a string argument (use `callframe.argument(0)` + `toSlice`)
- Parse INI line-by-line into JS objects directly:
  - Skip empty lines and comment lines (starting with `;` or `#`)
  - `[section]` headers: create/get a nested object on the result
  - `key=value` pairs: trim whitespace, strip quotes from values, detect booleans (`true`/`false`), `null`, and numbers
  - Support inline comments (`;` and `#` after values, respecting quoted strings)
  - Handle dotted section names like `[a.b]` as nested objects
- Return the JS object

**`stringify()` function:**
- Accept `(value, replacer, space)` arguments (same signature as JSON5/YAML)
- `replacer` is unsupported (throw if non-null, like JSON5)
- Iterate JS object properties:
  - First pass: emit all top-level non-object values as `key = value`
  - Second pass: emit all top-level object values as `[section]\nkey = value`
- Handle nested sections with dotted notation (`[a.b]`)
- String formatting: quote strings containing special chars (`=`, `;`, `#`, `[`, `]`, leading/trailing whitespace)
- Return the result via `StringBuilder.toString()`

### 2. Modify `src/bun.js/api.zig` (line ~34)

Add the import alongside the other format objects:
```zig
pub const INIObject = @import("./api/INIObject.zig");
```

### 3. Modify `src/bun.js/api/BunObject.zig` (3 locations)

**A. Lazy property declaration (~line 70, after YAML):**
```zig
pub const INI = toJSLazyPropertyCallback(Bun.getINIObject);
```

**B. Export in `exportAll()` (~line 140, after YAML):**
```zig
@export(&BunObject.INI, .{ .name = lazyPropertyCallbackName("INI") });
```

**C. Getter function (~line 1289, after getYAMLObject):**
```zig
pub fn getINIObject(globalThis: *jsc.JSGlobalObject, _: *jsc.JSObject) jsc.JSValue {
    return INIObject.create(globalThis);
}
```

**D. Import at bottom (~line 2083, after YAMLObject):**
```zig
const INIObject = bun.api.INIObject;
```

### 4. Modify `src/bun.js/bindings/BunObject+exports.h` (line ~25)

Add to `FOR_EACH_GETTER` macro (alphabetical order, after `Glob`):
```cpp
    macro(INI) \
```

### 5. Modify `src/bun.js/bindings/BunObject.cpp` (~line 968)

Add to `bunObjectTable` (after JSON5, before JSONL):
```cpp
    INI                                            BunObject_lazyPropCb_wrap_INI                                      DontDelete|PropertyCallback
```

### 6. Modify `packages/bun-types/bun.d.ts` (~after TOML namespace, line 794)

Add TypeScript definitions:
```typescript
  /**
   * INI related APIs
   */
  namespace INI {
    /**
     * Parse an INI string into a JavaScript object.
     *
     * Supports sections (as nested objects), key=value pairs,
     * and comment lines (`;` and `#`).
     *
     * @param input The INI string to parse
     * @returns A JavaScript object
     */
    export function parse(input: string): object;

    /**
     * Convert a JavaScript object into an INI string.
     *
     * @param input The JavaScript object to stringify
     * @param replacer Currently not supported.
     * @param space Indentation (number of spaces or string). Defaults to no extra indentation.
     * @returns An INI formatted string
     */
    export function stringify(input: object, replacer?: undefined | null, space?: string | number): string;
  }
```

### 7. Create `test/js/bun/ini/ini.test.ts` (NEW)

Test coverage for:
- **parse basics**: simple key=value, multiple keys, empty input
- **parse sections**: `[section]` creating nested objects, multiple sections
- **parse comments**: `;` and `#` comment lines skipped
- **parse value types**: strings, numbers, booleans (`true`/`false`), `null`
- **parse quoted values**: single and double quoted strings preserved
- **parse inline comments**: `key = value ; comment`
- **parse edge cases**: empty values, whitespace trimming, `=` in values, duplicate keys (last wins)
- **stringify basics**: flat object to INI
- **stringify sections**: nested objects become `[section]` blocks
- **stringify value types**: numbers, booleans, strings, null
- **stringify roundtrip**: `parse(stringify(obj))` equals original
- **error cases**: non-string input to parse, circular refs in stringify

## INI Parse Specification

```
; comment
# comment
key = value
key=value

[section]
key = value

[section.subsection]
key = value
```

- Lines starting with `;` or `#` are comments (after optional whitespace)
- `[name]` starts a section; keys after it go into a nested object
- `key = value` assigns a value; `=` is the delimiter
- Values are trimmed of whitespace; quoted values (`"..."` or `'...'`) have quotes stripped
- `true`, `false` parse as booleans; `null` parses as null
- Numeric strings that are valid numbers parse as numbers
- Inline comments: unquoted `;` or `#` after a value starts a comment

## INI Stringify Specification

- Top-level non-object values emitted first as `key = value`
- Top-level object values emitted as `[key]\n` followed by their key=value pairs
- Nested objects use dotted section names: `[parent.child]`
- Strings containing `=`, `;`, `#`, `[`, `]`, or leading/trailing whitespace are double-quoted
- Booleans emit `true`/`false`, null emits `null`, numbers emit their numeric representation
- Arrays are not natively supported in standard INI; stringify flat values only (skip arrays/nested-nested objects or emit them as JSON strings)

**`create()` function:**

- Register `parse` (arity 1) and `stringify` (arity 3) as JSFunctions
